questions.question: Is there a written clinical pharmacology plan aligned with the TPP?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates the existence and quality of a clinical pharmacology plan that aligns with clinical, regulatory, and commercial strategy. Assesses whether the plan outlines study objectives, key PK/PD analyses, timelines, go/no-go criteria, and integration with decision-making processes.
questions.rationale: A structured clinical pharmacology plan guides efficient evidence generation across development. It provides foresight into critical data needs, sequencing of studies, and alignment with regulatory milestones. Lack of a coherent plan increases the risk of missed opportunities, misaligned trials, and data gaps at pivotal decision points.

questions.question: Is the dose selection strategy clear and justified across development stages?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether dose selection is supported by an integrated analysis of pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, exposure–response relationships, and relevant modeling or simulation. Evaluates the quantitative justification for first-in-human, proof-of-concept, and pivotal study doses.
questions.rationale: Robust dose justification ensures patient safety and optimizes the therapeutic index. A data-driven approach supports regulatory confidence, enables efficient trial design, and reduces the likelihood of failed efficacy or safety outcomes. Poorly justified doses can lead to regulatory holds, underpowered trials, or post-approval failures.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology activities integrated into the overall development plan?
questions.rubric_tests: Examines how clinical pharmacology deliverables are coordinated with clinical operations, regulatory, and CMC timelines. Assesses visibility of pharmacology milestones in the overall development strategy and governance reviews.
questions.rationale: Cross-functional integration ensures pharmacology data are available at critical junctures—such as dose selection, protocol amendments, or regulatory submissions. It enables proactive planning for go/no-go decisions and strengthens cross-team alignment. Lack of integration can delay programs or lead to misaligned regulatory interactions.

questions.question: Are PK profiles adequately characterized in healthy subjects?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether single- and multiple-dose pharmacokinetics are described using noncompartmental and/or population PK methods. Includes assessment of absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination (ADME), and variability in relevant populations.
questions.rationale: Comprehensive PK characterization informs dose selection, exposure margins, and safety monitoring. It supports modeling, exposure-response analyses, and regulatory decision-making across phases. Inadequate PK data jeopardize trial design, limit generalizability, and may trigger regulatory concerns.

questions.question: Has dose proportionality been assessed across the therapeutic range?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether systemic exposure increases linearly with dose, based on Cmax and AUC relationships. Evaluates the statistical and graphical methods used, and whether findings inform dosing rationale and safety margins.
questions.rationale: Dose proportionality supports predictable PK behavior and simplifies titration and labeling strategies. Nonlinearity may signal saturation, altered metabolism, or transporter effects requiring further investigation. Clear characterization avoids ambiguity in clinical trial dosing and regulatory submissions.

questions.question: Are single- and multiple-dose PKs evaluated?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether both single-dose and repeated-dose pharmacokinetics have been evaluated to characterize absorption, clearance, and time-dependent changes. Examines whether steady-state kinetics and accumulation ratios are quantified and integrated into dose justification.
questions.rationale: Evaluating single- and multiple-dose PKs is critical to understand linearity, predict steady-state behavior, and anticipate accumulation. This informs dose regimen selection, especially for chronic administration. Lack of these data may result in suboptimal dosing schedules or safety concerns.

questions.question: Is accumulation and time to steady state known?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the accumulation profile and time to steady state are adequately characterized based on half-life and dosing frequency. Includes assessment of time-dependent changes in metabolism or clearance and impact on exposure.
questions.rationale: Understanding drug accumulation and time to steady state ensures appropriate therapeutic monitoring and helps define loading dose needs, dosing intervals, and trial washout periods. Overlooking these can increase risk of toxicity or misinterpret time-dependent efficacy signals.

questions.question: Are inter-individual and intra-individual variabilities quantified?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether PK variability within and between subjects has been quantified through statistical modeling or visual diagnostics. Evaluates relevance to dose adjustments, risk management, and trial population selection.
questions.rationale: Quantifying variability informs clinical trial design and labeling by identifying subgroups requiring dose adjustment or monitoring. Failure to assess variability may lead to inconsistent efficacy, safety concerns, or regulatory pushback on dosing flexibility.

questions.question: Are there validated PD biomarkers linked to MoA or clinical endpoints?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether pharmacodynamic biomarkers are available and validated to reflect target engagement or biological activity. Includes relevance to mechanism of action (MoA), disease modulation, or downstream clinical effects.
questions.rationale: Validated PD biomarkers offer early indicators of drug activity and support dose selection and proof-of-concept studies. They reduce development risk by enabling go/no-go decisions and mechanistic insight. Without validated PD markers, programs may lack early confidence in effect.

questions.question: Is there an E-R model for efficacy? For safety?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether exposure–response (E–R) models are available for both efficacy and safety endpoints. Evaluates model robustness, data sufficiency, and use in dose selection or regulatory submission.
questions.rationale: E–R models for efficacy and safety underpin rational dose selection, help define the therapeutic window, and guide labeling. They are often requested by regulators to justify pivotal dosing. Absence of E–R modeling limits precision in balancing risk and benefit.

questions.question: Has a therapeutic index been established or estimated?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the margin between efficacious and toxic exposures is quantified, using exposure–response models, nonclinical safety margins, or early human data. Assesses if therapeutic index is being used to guide dose justification and risk management.
questions.rationale: Defining the therapeutic index is fundamental to balancing benefit and risk, selecting starting doses, and informing labeling language. A narrow therapeutic index often necessitates therapeutic drug monitoring or risk mitigation strategies. Failure to define it may result in regulatory scrutiny or safety-related delays.

questions.question: Are E-R analyses used to support dose selection?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether exposure–response (E–R) relationships have been applied prospectively or retrospectively to inform dosing decisions for various patient groups. Includes use in pivotal trials, labeling discussions, and safety margin justifications.
questions.rationale: Using E–R data to support dose selection increases confidence in clinical trial outcomes and regulatory acceptance. It facilitates rational justification of posology and reduces the risk of late-stage dosing surprises. Regulators expect this data to support effectiveness and safety claims.

questions.question: Has absolute bioavailability been measured?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the absolute bioavailability of the active ingredient has been quantified, usually via IV reference studies. Assesses impact on systemic exposure, formulation strategy, and first-pass metabolism understanding.
questions.rationale: Absolute bioavailability informs how much of the drug reaches systemic circulation, guiding formulation optimization, bridging strategies, and potential for non-oral routes. Omitting this step may obscure dosing assumptions or formulation changes in development.

questions.question: Are formulation changes (e.g. Phase 1 to Phase 3) bridged by data?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether formulation transitions are supported by PK bridging data, demonstrating comparability in exposure and performance. Includes scale-up and manufacturing changes that may impact bioavailability.
questions.rationale: Unjustified formulation transitions may confound efficacy or safety interpretation across trials. Bridging studies ensure continuity in exposure and allow valid comparisons. This is a key regulatory expectation during lifecycle development.

questions.question: Has the effect of food on PK been assessed?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether a food effect study has been conducted to assess changes in absorption, exposure, and variability under fed versus fasted conditions. Assesses design (high-fat vs. moderate meal), population, and regulatory alignment.
questions.rationale: Food can significantly impact bioavailability, absorption rate, and safety. Understanding these effects informs dosing instructions, label language, and trial conduct. Failure to assess food effect may lead to dosing inconsistency or require labeling revisions.

questions.question: Are BA/BE data adequate to support regulatory and commercial formulations?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether bioavailability (BA) or bioequivalence (BE) data are sufficient to support the intended clinical and commercial formulations. Evaluates study design, sensitivity, and comparability across dosage forms and strengths.
questions.rationale: BA/BE data are essential to confirm formulation performance and enable regulatory approval without repeat efficacy trials. Lack of adequate BA/BE data can trigger regulatory rejection, require reformulation, or delay market entry.

questions.question: Is the metabolic and transporter pathway well-characterized?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the primary metabolic enzymes (e.g., CYP450 isoforms) and transporters (e.g., P-gp, BCRP) involved in disposition are identified and quantified. Assesses use of in vitro and in vivo studies to map pathways relevant to interactions and population variability.
questions.rationale: Understanding metabolism and transport is key to predicting drug interactions, variability across populations, and the impact of comedications. Insufficient characterization may lead to overlooked safety risks or missed opportunities for dose individualization.

questions.question: Has the compound been evaluated for CYP induction/inhibition?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether the compound has been tested in vitro and/or in vivo for potential to induce or inhibit cytochrome P450 enzymes. Evaluates alignment with ICH M12 and FDA DDI guidance, and adequacy of labeling implications.
questions.rationale: CYP inhibition or induction can dramatically alter drug exposure, leading to safety or efficacy risks in co-administered therapies. Early testing ensures appropriate risk mitigation, including label warnings or contraindications.

questions.question: Are clinical DDI studies conducted or planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether clinical drug–drug interaction (DDI) studies have been performed for key perpetrator or victim interactions. Assesses prioritization, design quality, and integration with in vitro data and PBPK modeling.
questions.rationale: Clinical DDI studies provide real-world confirmation of interaction risks, informing labeling, trial exclusion criteria, and treatment algorithms. Failure to conduct necessary studies may lead to delayed approval or restricted label use.

questions.question: Is PBPK modeling used to predict untested interactions?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models have been used to simulate potential drug interactions when clinical studies are infeasible or unethical. Evaluates model quality, verification, and regulatory engagement.
questions.rationale: PBPK modeling allows prediction of interactions in special populations or rare combinations. It is increasingly accepted by regulators when supported by robust data. Its use can reduce development burden and anticipate safety signals.

questions.question: Are DDI labeling statements supported by data or modeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether labeling recommendations related to drug–drug interactions (DDIs) are justified by clinical studies, in vitro data, or validated PBPK models. Assesses if conclusions are consistent with regulatory guidance and labeling templates.
questions.rationale: DDI-related labeling affects prescriber behavior, patient safety, and commercial uptake. Unsupported or overly cautious labeling can limit use, while under-characterized risks may lead to post-market actions. Strong data or modeling improves confidence in labeling claims.

questions.question: Renal impairment: Is a study conducted or waived with justification?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether renal impairment studies (mild/moderate/severe) have been conducted or if a waiver is appropriately justified based on clearance mechanism, safety, or modeling.
questions.rationale: Renal function impacts drug exposure for renally cleared compounds. Regulators require data or a strong justification for waiver to ensure dosing guidance in this population. Omission without rationale may delay approval or restrict labeling.

questions.question: Hepatic impairment: Are PKs studied in mild/moderate/severe dysfunction?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether dedicated hepatic impairment studies have been conducted, including stratification by liver function (Child-Pugh A/B/C). Considers study quality, endpoints, and labeling impact.
questions.rationale: Hepatic metabolism affects exposure and risk in liver-impaired patients. Data are essential for dosing guidance and often required by regulators. Inadequate characterization can lead to usage restrictions or additional commitments.

questions.question: Age, sex, body weight: Are covariate effects modeled (e.g., via PopPK)?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether intrinsic factors such as age, sex, and weight have been explored via population PK modeling or stratified analyses. Reviews how findings influence dosing guidance or labeling.
questions.rationale: These covariates contribute to variability in exposure. Modeling helps define whether dosing adjustments are necessary and supports claims of broad applicability. Failure to assess these factors weakens regulatory confidence in generalizability.

questions.question: Race/ethnicity: Are there adequate PK data in relevant global populations?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether PK data are available in racially and ethnically diverse populations, especially if global markets are targeted. Considers whether observed differences impact exposure or dosing.
questions.rationale: Racial and ethnic differences in metabolism and response can affect efficacy, safety, and acceptance in specific markets. Global regulators and payers often expect representative data. Failure to account for this may restrict global launch or reimbursement.

questions.question: Pediatrics: Is there a pediatric PK plan (e.g., iPSP, extrapolation modeling)?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether pediatric pharmacokinetic strategies are in place, including initial Pediatric Study Plans (iPSP), modeling approaches (e.g., allometric scaling, PBPK), and staged study designs.
questions.rationale: Pediatric PK planning is critical for regulatory approval and ethical development in children. Early modeling and structured plans de-risk development and meet global regulatory requirements (e.g., FDA, EMA). Omission may delay pediatric submissions or breach pediatric study obligations.

questions.question: Elderly: Are elderly patients included or studied in trials?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates inclusion of elderly patients in PK studies or broader clinical trials and analysis of age-related changes in PK/PD. Reviews how results inform dosing or monitoring in geriatric populations.
questions.rationale: The elderly often exhibit altered clearance, comorbidities, and polypharmacy, affecting drug exposure and response. Regulatory guidance expects characterization in this population. Inadequate data can limit safe use or reduce payer confidence.

questions.question: Pregnancy/lactation: Are risks and PK changes modeled or studied?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether pregnancy-related physiological changes and lactation exposure are modeled or studied. Includes placental transfer data, exposure timing, and breast milk excretion analysis.
questions.rationale: Pregnancy and lactation alter PK and safety profiles. Modeling or empirical data support regulatory decisions, labeling, and risk mitigation. This is increasingly expected, especially for women-targeted therapies. Lack of data can result in “not recommended” labeling.

questions.question: Is a PopPK model developed, validated, and used for decision-making?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) models are constructed with appropriate structure, covariates, diagnostics, and predictive checks. Assesses if they are applied to support dose selection and regulatory filings.
questions.rationale: PopPK modeling allows integration of sparse and diverse data to guide dose decisions, explore covariate effects, and simulate scenarios. Regulators rely on these models for special population dosing and labeling. Poorly built or unvalidated models reduce credibility and utility.

questions.question: Are covariates (e.g., body weight, renal function) incorporated into dosing guidance?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether significant covariates identified in PopPK models are used to develop individualized or stratified dosing recommendations. Includes threshold effects, exposure targets, and label impact.
questions.rationale: Translating covariate effects into practical dosing guidance enhances safety, efficacy, and regulatory approval. It also supports personalization. Without integration, covariate analysis remains academic and fails to influence clinical practice or labeling.

questions.question: Are model files available for inspection (e.g., NONMEM, R)?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether raw model files, scripts, and outputs from tools like NONMEM, Monolix, or R are archived, accessible, and reproducible. Evaluates file organization, annotations, and readiness for regulator or partner inspection.
questions.rationale: Transparent and well-documented model files are critical for reproducibility, regulatory review, and audit readiness. Lack of access or unclear documentation can undermine confidence in modeling outcomes and delay approvals or due diligence processes.

questions.question: Are simulations supporting dose selection and labeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether simulation outputs (e.g., virtual trials, exposure distributions) are used to inform dosing strategies, label recommendations, or trial design. Reviews methods, scenarios, and regulatory alignment.
questions.rationale: Simulations can pre-emptively test dosing hypotheses and reduce risk in trials. They are powerful tools to justify dose in special populations or bridge between formulations. When omitted, labeling may be more conservative or trial design less efficient.

questions.question: Is PBPK used to support waivers or DDI predictions?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether physiologically-based pharmacokinetic models are used to justify waivers (e.g., for food effect, organ impairment) or predict DDIs. Evaluates biological plausibility, verification, and adherence to regulatory guidance (e.g., FDA, EMA).
questions.rationale: PBPK modeling enables mechanistic predictions when clinical studies are impractical. Regulatory agencies increasingly accept them when robust and validated. Strategic use reduces study burden and enhances regulatory confidence in extrapolated claims.

questions.question: Are PBPK models built with adequate quality and documentation?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses structural model design, input assumptions, data sources, verification, and traceability. Includes platform used (e.g., Simcyp, GastroPlus), model validation steps, and version control.
questions.rationale: Poorly justified or undocumented PBPK models may be dismissed by regulators or internal reviewers. High-quality documentation supports regulatory filings, due diligence, and scientific credibility. It also ensures model reusability across programs.

questions.question: Are PBPK models submitted to regulators or part of pre-IND/IND packages?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether PBPK models were submitted to support drug development decisions, DDI risk assessment, or special population dosing. Reviews their role in formal interactions (e.g., FDA pre-IND meetings).
questions.rationale: Regulatory submission of PBPK models demonstrates maturity, scientific rigor, and confidence. Their inclusion facilitates earlier alignment and can substitute for clinical data. Failure to leverage models in submissions may be a missed opportunity to reduce burden or accelerate development.

questions.question: Is a Pediatric Study Plan (iPSP / PIP / PSP) in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether a formal pediatric development plan (e.g., iPSP for FDA, PIP for EMA) has been submitted, reviewed, or agreed upon. Assesses plan content, timelines, and justification for deferrals or waivers.
questions.rationale: Regulatory authorities require early pediatric planning to support future labeling and market access. A clear, timely plan ensures alignment with agency expectations and prevents delays or noncompliance with pediatric obligations.

questions.question: Are regulatory interactions (pre-IND, EOP2, Scientific Advice) documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses documentation and outcomes from key regulatory milestones involving clinical pharmacology topics. Includes pre-IND, End-of-Phase 2 (EOP2), and Scientific Advice meetings.
questions.rationale: Capturing and responding to regulatory feedback is vital to align pharmacology plans with agency expectations. Poor documentation or missed opportunities to engage can result in gaps at NDA/BLA filing or rejected modeling strategies.

questions.question: Is the clinical pharmacology package adequate to support dose justification in pivotal studies?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the totality of PK, PD, exposure–response, and modeling data sufficiently justify the dose(s) used in Phase 3. Includes data coherence, regulatory acceptability, and risk mitigation.
questions.rationale: Inadequate justification of pivotal doses can derail approvals or prompt additional studies. A comprehensive and integrated package strengthens the credibility of efficacy/safety findings and facilitates smoother regulatory review.

questions.question: Is there a clear relationship between exposure and AEs?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether exposure–adverse event (E–AE) relationships have been explored using clinical or model-based approaches. Includes analysis of thresholds, dose-response, and time-dependent toxicity.
questions.rationale: Understanding E–AE relationships helps optimize dose, define monitoring, and inform risk–benefit assessments. Lack of clarity on exposure-driven toxicities can result in labeling constraints or post-market requirements.

questions.question: Are there modeling-based safety margins for critical toxicities?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether quantitative safety margins have been modeled to define acceptable exposure ranges. Includes integration with toxicokinetic (TK) data and clinical PK/PD findings.
questions.rationale: Modeling safety margins is essential for dose justification, trial safety monitoring, and regulatory decision-making. Absence of modeled safety thresholds limits confidence in dose flexibility and may require conservative default strategies.

questions.question: Are PK/PD/clinical data integrated to support risk–benefit claims?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, efficacy, and safety data are quantitatively integrated (e.g., exposure–response models, therapeutic index estimation) to support the target dose and risk–benefit profile.
questions.rationale: Integrated modeling strengthens the scientific justification for dose selection and therapeutic margins. It provides a coherent narrative for regulators and investors. Weak integration can raise concerns about data maturity or the strength of benefit-risk conclusions.

questions.question: Is the current dataset adequate to support labeling claims (DDIs, special populations, food effect)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates completeness and relevance of the clinical pharmacology data package as it relates to final label content. Reviews whether studies on DDIs, intrinsic/extrinsic factors, and food effect are adequate in scope and quality.
questions.rationale: Clinical pharmacology claims are critical for prescribers and regulators. Incomplete or poorly designed data can lead to restrictive labeling, delayed approval, or post-market commitments. Adequacy of the data package ensures defensibility of label language.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology sections of the IB and draft label consistent?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether investigational brochure (IB), proposed labeling, and regulatory documentation present a coherent and harmonized clinical pharmacology narrative.
questions.rationale: Inconsistencies across documents create confusion and undermine credibility with regulators and partners. Ensuring alignment demonstrates quality control and supports clear communication of PK/PD, DDIs, and population-specific considerations.

questions.question: Are gaps expected to be acceptable or flagged by regulatory authorities?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether any deficiencies in the clinical pharmacology package (e.g., missing data, modeling limitations) are well-characterized, transparently acknowledged, and expected to be accepted by regulators.
questions.rationale: Foreseeing and managing acceptable gaps reflects maturity of regulatory strategy. Unanticipated deficiencies can trigger delays or rejections. This rubric tests for anticipatory planning and realistic risk mitigation around data shortfalls.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology study reports complete and accessible?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether reports for all key studies (e.g., BA/BE, DDI, renal/hepatic impairment, food effect) are finalized, archived, and easily retrievable for inspection or submission.
questions.rationale: Report completeness and accessibility are essential for due diligence, regulatory submissions, and audits. Missing or disorganized documents create delays and undermine confidence in the development program.

questions.question: Are datasets (e.g., SDTM, ADaM) and model files well-documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Assesses whether analysis-ready datasets and modeling files are organized, annotated, version-controlled, and aligned with CDISC standards. Includes evaluation of metadata availability and reproducibility.
questions.rationale: High-quality documentation of datasets and models ensures regulatory compliance, reproducibility, and enables rapid response to queries. Poor documentation slows submissions, undermines confidence in data integrity, and may prompt regulatory audits or reanalysis.

questions.question: Is there a data room with sufficient documentation to enable investor/regulator review?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether a virtual data room or secure platform is in place with key clinical pharmacology files accessible for due diligence or regulatory review. Includes clarity of structure, permissions, and document traceability.
questions.rationale: A well-prepared data room accelerates external assessments, enhances transparency, and signals organizational maturity. Lack of structured access delays reviews and may be perceived as risk during fundraising, partnership, or regulatory interactions.
